## Long Rest

A long rest is **declared during the Card Selection step** and constitutes the character's **entire turn for the round** (no cards are played). It is performed on **initiative 99**.

When a character long rests, they follow these steps **in order**:

1. **Lose one card of their choice** from their discard pile and return the remaining cards to their hand. *(Mandatory)*
2. **Perform *Heal 2*, self.** *(Optional)*
3. **Recover all spent items.** Items can even be used during the same turn in which they are recovered. *(Optional)*

---

A couple of important notes:
- A long rest can only be performed if the character has **at least two cards** in their discard pile (since one card must be lost).
- Because it happens at initiative 99, enemies and other characters will almost certainly act **before** you long rest.
- Unlike a short rest, you get to **choose** which card you lose — there's no random element.

*(Source: Frosthaven Rulebook)*

2. **Perform "*Heal 2*, self"** *(optional)*.
3. **Recover all of their spent items** *(optional)*. Items can be used during the same turn in which they are recovered.

Note that, like a short rest, a long rest can only be performed if the character has at least two cards in their discard pile.

## Recommended Scenario Level

> At recommended difficulty, the scenario level is equal to the **average level of the characters divided by 2 (rounded up).**

**Example from the rulebook:** If all characters are level 2, the average level (2) ÷ 2 = 1, so the scenario is still **level 1**. Only once a character reaches level 3 would the scenario level potentially increase.

---

### Adjusting the Level

The recommended calculation is just a starting point. At the start of **any** scenario, you can freely set the scenario level to any number from **0–7**:
- **Decrease it** if your party is struggling.
- **Increase it** if you want more of a challenge as your party grows stronger.

---

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monster Level | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Gold Conversion | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 4 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| Trap Damage | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Hazardous Terrain | 1 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 4 |
| Bonus XP | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 | 14 | 16 | 18 |

*(Source: Frosthaven Rulebook)*

The rules text was found in the first result. Here's what *Poison* does in Frosthaven:

---

**Poison** (Negative Condition)

> **Poison:** All attacks targeting the figure gain "+1 Attack." Poison is removed when the figure is healed but, unlike *Wound*/*Brittle*/*Bane*, poison prevents the heal from increasing the figure's current hit point value.

*(Rulebook, p. 28 — Negative Conditions)*

---

### Summary of key points:

- **Offensive debuff:** Every attack made against a *Poisoned* figure gets **+1 Attack**, making them easier to damage.
- **Removal:** *Poison* is removed when the figure receives a *Heal* effect — but **the heal does not actually restore any hit points**. The condition consumes the heal entirely.
- This is different from *Wound*, *Brittle*, and *Bane*, which are also removed by healing but still allow the hit point restoration to go through.

So *Poison* is both a threat multiplier (enemies hit harder against you) and a healing trap (you must spend a heal just to remove it, gaining no HP in return).

Here's what *Poison* does in Frosthaven:

> "All attacks targeting the figure gain '+1 Attack.' Poison is removed when the figure is healed but, unlike wound/brittle/bane, poison prevents the heal from increasing the figure's current hit point value."

Key points:
- While poisoned, every attack against the figure deals +1 damage.
- *Poison* is a negative condition, so it can't be removed voluntarily — it lingers until the figure is healed.
- When a heal removes *Poison*, that heal does **not** restore any hit points; it only clears the condition. (Other heals from the same ability or other sources can still restore HP normally once *Poison* is gone.)
- A figure can't have multiple stacks of *Poison*; reapplying it just refreshes the condition.

Source: Frosthaven Rulebook, Negative Conditions section (p. 32, rulebook chunk 101).

Here is what the Frosthaven Rulebook says about the *Brittle* condition:

> **Brittle:** The next time the figure would suffer damage from any source, they instead suffer double that amount of damage, and *Brittle* is then removed. *Brittle* is also removed if the figure is healed.

*(Rulebook, Negative Conditions section)*

### Key points:
- *Brittle* **doubles the very next instance of damage** the affected figure takes, from **any source** (attacks, traps, terrain, abilities, etc.).
- After that doubled damage is applied, *Brittle* is **immediately removed**.
- *Brittle* is also removed if the figure is **healed**, even if no damage has been taken yet.
- It is a **negative condition**, so it is typically applied to enemies (or occasionally characters) by specific abilities.

Here is the complete breakdown of how *Advantage* (and *Disadvantage*) works with attack modifier cards in Frosthaven, directly from the Rulebook (chunk 95–96):

---

## Advantage

> With advantage, the attacker draws two modifiers and uses one of them. A monster always uses the better one, but a **character may use either one.**

So players have a small strategic choice — you aren't forced to take the numerically better card.

---

## Disadvantage

> With disadvantage, the attacker draws two modifiers and always uses the worse one.

No choice here — you must use the worse result.

---

## Interaction with Rolling Modifiers

This is where it gets more involved:

> If the first draw with advantage or disadvantage is a *rolling modifier*, the attacker draws additional modifiers, one at a time, until a non-rolling modifier is drawn. They then draw one more modifier and ignore any rolling icon on it.

The comparison is then made between those **last two** non-rolling draws:

- **Advantage:** Keep all initial rolling modifiers **plus** whichever of the final two cards you choose.
- **Disadvantage:** **Ignore** all initial rolling modifiers and use whichever of the final two is worse.

> If the first draw with advantage or disadvantage is **not** a rolling modifier, but the second draw **is**, the rolling icon on the second modifier is still ignored.

---

## Ambiguity

> When there is ambiguity about which modifier is worse, the attacker must use the one drawn first. Ambiguity occurs when comparing the non-numeric effects of some modifiers (e.g., elemental infusions or negative conditions). Any non-numeric effect is considered to have a positive but undefined value.

---

## Cancellation

> An attack cannot gain multiple instances of advantage or disadvantage. If an attack has both advantage and disadvantage, it is considered to have neither.

---

## Bonus Rule: Ranged Attacks on Adjacent Enemies

> Any ranged attack on an adjacent enemy automatically gains *Disadvantage*.

*(Rulebook, p. 26)*

Here's how *advantage* (and *disadvantage*) works with attack modifier cards in Frosthaven:

## Basic rule
- **With *advantage*:** Draw two attack modifier cards and use one of them.
  - A **monster** always uses the better one.
  - A **character** may use either one.
- **With *disadvantage*:** Draw two modifiers and always use the worse one.

## When *rolling modifiers* are involved
If the first draw is a *rolling modifier*, keep drawing one at a time until a non-rolling modifier comes up. Then draw **one more** modifier, and ignore any rolling icon on that final card. The first non-rolling modifier and the one after it are the two compared:
- **Advantage:** The attacker uses **all** the initial rolling modifiers, plus whichever of the last two compared cards they choose.
- **Disadvantage:** The attacker **ignores** all the initial rolling modifiers and uses whichever of the last two compared cards is worse.

If the first draw is *not* a rolling modifier but the second one is, the rolling icon on that second card is still ignored.

## Edge cases
- **Ambiguity:** When it's unclear which modifier is worse (e.g., comparing non-numeric effects like elemental infusions or negative conditions), the attacker must use the one drawn first. Any non-numeric effect counts as a positive but undefined value.
- **Stacking:** An attack can't gain multiple instances of *advantage* or *disadvantage*. If an attack has both, it's treated as having neither.
- **Adjacent ranged attacks:** Any ranged attack on an adjacent enemy automatically gains *disadvantage*.

Source: Frosthaven Rulebook, "Advantage and Disadvantage" section (p. 25 area, rulebook chunks 95–96).

When an attack gains *advantage*, the attacker **draws two modifier cards and uses one of them**:
- A **monster always uses the better one**

1. The attacker draws additional modifiers **one at a time** until a non-rolling modifier is drawn

- An attack **cannot gain multiple instances** of advantage or disadvantage
- If an attack has **both advantage and disadvantage, it is considered to have neither**
- When comparing modifiers is ambiguous (e.g., comparing elemental infusions or negative conditions), the modifier drawn first must be used

In *Frosthaven*, *advantage*/*disadvantage* affects how you draw and choose **attack modifier cards** for an attack.

### Advantage (attack has advantage)
- When you perform the attack, **draw two attack modifier cards**.
- **Use one of them** (the attacker chooses which one to apply).
- A **monster always uses the better** of the two.
- Then resolve that chosen modifier’s effects, and discard it.

### Disadvantage
- Draw **two attack modifier cards**.
- **Use the worse one** (monster still uses the better, but disadvantage makes them use the worse?—per the rule: *with disadvantage, the attacker… always uses the worse one*).

### Rolling modifier timing (important edge case)
- If the **first** modifier drawn (with advantage/disadvantage) is **rolling**, you keep drawing more modifiers **until you get a non-rolling** one.
- Then you draw **one more modifier** (so you end up comparing the **last two non-ignored** modifiers):
  - **Advantage:** use **all initial rolling modifiers** *and* choose whichever of the **last two** you want.
  - **Disadvantage:** ignore the **initial rolling modifiers** and use whichever of the **last two** is worse.
- If a rolling icon appears on the “second draw” in a specific non-rolling/rolling combination, that rolling icon is **still ignored** (per the rulebook clarification).

### Multiple instances & conflict
- An attack **cannot gain multiple instances** of advantage or disadvantage.
- If the attack has **both advantage and disadvantage**, treat it as **neither** (normal draw/resolve behavior).

Source: *Frosthaven Rulebook*, section covering *Advantage and Disadvantage* and *Attack modifier cards* (shown in the indexed “Sticker 5” rule text).

The rulebook's **Item Limits** section gives the answer, and it's level-dependent for *Small* (*I*) items specifically:

> Each character can bring one **Head** (E) item, one **Body** (B) item, one **Feet** (L) item, up to two **One Hand** (H) items *-or-* one **Two Hands** (h) item, and a number of **Small** (I) items **up to half their level (rounded up).**

So the limit for *Small* items scales with character level:

| Character Level | Max *Small* Items |
|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1 |
| 2–3 | 1–2 |
| 4–5 | 2–3 |
| 6–7 | 3–4 |
| 8–9 | 4–5 |

*(Half the level, rounded up)*

For example, a **level 1** character can bring **1** *Small* item, a **level 4** character can bring **2**, and a **level 9** character can bring **5**.

Here's a full summary of how looting works in Frosthaven, based on the Rulebook (chunks 110–112):

---

## Looting

### The *Loot* Ability
*Loot X* is an ability that allows a figure to loot all loot tokens and treasure tiles within range X, including any in their current hex. It is unaffected by the presence of figures or overlay tiles. If there are no valid targets within range, the ability **cannot** be performed.

---

### Looting Loot Tokens
When a loot token is looted, it is removed from the map.
- **Monsters:** Nothing else happens — they gain no benefit.
- **Characters:** Draw one card from the loot deck (unless it's empty).
  - If the card provides a **random item**, check it off in the scenario's loot table and draw from the random item deck. The item can be used immediately for the rest of the scenario, even if it exceeds your item limit. If the random item deck is empty, treat it as a money card with three coins.
  - If the card provides **money or resources**, the character does **not** gain them until the **end of the scenario**.

---

### Looting Treasure Tiles
When a treasure tile is looted, it is removed from the map.
- **Monsters cannot loot treasure tiles.**
- If it is a **numbered treasure**, look it up in the Treasure Index and apply the effect.
- Only the looting character gains the reward — **except** for random item blueprints or random scenarios, which may be shared.
- If a character gains an item they cannot keep for any reason, they must give it to another character or sell it immediately.

---

### End-of-Turn Looting
Characters automatically loot loot tokens in their hex at the end of their turn (this is separate from the *Loot* ability). **Monsters and summons do not perform end-of-turn looting.**

---

*(Rulebook, pp. ~38–39)*

# Looting in Frosthaven

Looting is how characters pick up loot tokens and treasure tiles from the map during a scenario. It's done via the *Loot X* ability (or by end-of-turn looting).

**The Loot ability** (Rulebook):

> "*Loot X*" is an ability that allows a figure to loot all loot tokens and treasure tiles within range X, including any in their current hex. This ability is unaffected by the presence of figures or overlay tiles. If there are no loot tokens or treasure tiles within the specified range, the ability cannot be performed.

**What happens when you loot:**

- **Loot tokens** — Removed from the map. If a character looted it, they draw one card from the **loot deck**:
  - A **random item** card lets the character check off a *random item* (×1) on the scenario's loot table, then draw from the random item deck and immediately use that item for the rest of the scenario (even over their item limit). If the deck is empty, the card becomes a money card worth 3 coins.
  - **Money or resource** cards aren't gained until the end of the scenario.
- **Treasure tiles** — Removed from the map. If it's a numbered treasure, look up the number in the Treasure Index and apply the effect. Only the looting character gets the reward, unless it's a *random item* blueprint or *random scenario*. **Monsters cannot loot treasure tiles.**

**End-of-turn looting:** At the end of a character's turn, they automatically loot any loot tokens (not treasure tiles) in their current hex. **Monsters and summons do not perform end-of-turn looting** (Rulebook, Appendix C).

**Item conflicts:** If a character would gain an item they can't legally hold, they must immediately give it to another character or sell it (see Rulebook p. 67).

Sources: Rulebook passages 111–112, 222.

If there are no loot tokens or treasure tiles within the specified range, the ability cannot be performed.

When looting happens:
- **For monsters:** The loot token is removed from the map and nothing else happens.
- **For characters:** When a loot token is looted, the character draws one card from the loot deck (unless it's empty). If the loot card provides:
  - A **random item:** The character draws from the random item deck and adds it to their pool of items for the rest of the scenario (they can use it even if it puts them above the normal item limit). If the random item deck is empty, the card counts as money with three coins instead.
  - **Money or resources:** The character does not gain them until the end of the scenario.

When a treasure tile is looted, it is removed from the map. Note that **monsters cannot loot treasure tiles**—only characters can.

In *Frosthaven*, **looting** is the process of gaining loot from the scenario during play.

- The rules define an ability called **“Loot X”**: it lets a figure loot **all *loot tokens* and *treasure tiles* within range X** (including any in the figure’s current hex). If there are none in range, the ability can’t be performed.  
- When you loot:
  - **Loot tokens** are removed from the map.  
    - If a **monster** loots, nothing else happens.  
    - If a **character** loots, they **draw 1 card from the loot deck** (if it isn’t empty). What that card is determines what the character gains.
  - **Treasure tiles** are removed from the map. **Monsters can’t loot treasure tiles.**

- In some scenarios (e.g., crate-based ones), looting can also be done by **forgoing your bottom action to loot an adjacent crate**, and the “looted crates” are placed on the looting character’s mat.

Here's what the Frosthaven Rulebook says about the *Wound* condition:

> **Wound:** The figure suffers 1 damage at the start of each of their turns. *Wound* is removed when the figure is healed.

*(Rulebook, p. 29 — Negative Conditions)*

**Key points to keep in mind:**

- *Wound* is a **negative condition** that deals **1 damage at the start of each turn** of the afflicted figure — whether that's a hero or a monster.
- It persists until the figure is **healed** (by any *Heal* effect). It does **not** expire on its own at the end of a turn like some other conditions.
- If a figure has both *Regenerate* and *Wound*, the *Regenerate* effect ("*Heal* 1, self") is applied **first**, which removes *Wound* before it can deal its damage.

Per the Frosthaven Rulebook (Negative Conditions):

> *Wound*: The figure suffers 1 damage at the start of each of their turns. *Wound* is removed when the figure is healed.

A couple of useful interactions from the same rules section:

- If a figure has both *Regenerate* and *Wound*, the *Regenerate* effect is applied first, which heals the figure and thereby removes *Wound* before it would deal damage (Rulebook, Positive Conditions).
- Because healing removes *Wound*, even a *Heal 1* is enough to clear it.

Source: Frosthaven Rulebook, Conditions reference (p. 29 / sticker reference page).

> The figure suffers 1 damage at the start of each of their turns. Wound is removed when the figure is healed.

In other words, whenever a figure with the *Wound* condition starts their turn, they automatically take 1 damage. The condition persists until the figure receives healing.

Here are the rules for *Brittle*, quoted directly from the Frosthaven Rulebook (chunk 99, Negative Conditions section):

> **Brittle:** The next time the figure would suffer damage from any source, they instead suffer double that amount of damage, and *Brittle* is then removed. *Brittle* is also removed if the figure is healed.

**Source:** Frosthaven Rulebook — Negative Conditions section.

---

A few key points to keep in mind:

- *Brittle* **doubles all damage** from the very next damage instance, regardless of the source (attack, hazardous terrain, etc.).
- It is a **one-time effect** — it is removed immediately after that doubled damage is applied.
- It is also removed by any **healing** effect, even before damage is taken.
- As with all conditions, *Brittle* **cannot be removed voluntarily** and **cannot stack** — gaining it again while already *Brittle* simply resets its duration.
- Damage from *Ward* or *Brittle* is applied **before** a character decides how to handle the damage (Rulebook, chunk 125).

A couple of related notes from the rulebook on how *Brittle* interacts with damage:

- *Brittle* (and *Ward*) is applied **before** the character resolves how to handle the damage. From the Character Damage rules: "When a character would suffer any amount of damage (after ward or brittle is applied), they must either reduce their red hit point dial by that amount or negate the damage." (Rulebook, chunk 125)
- General condition rules apply: a figure can't have multiple instances of the same condition, but re-applying *Brittle* resets its duration; and if an attack adds *Brittle* as an added effect, the target gains it even if the attack dealt no damage — unless the attack killed/exhausted them or they're immune. (Rulebook, chunk 97)

## *Brittle* — How It Works

*(Source: Frosthaven Rulebook, chunk 99)*

> **Brittle:** The next time the figure would suffer damage from any source, they instead suffer double that amount of damage, and *Brittle* is then removed. *Brittle* is also removed if the figure is healed.

Key points for the table:

- **Doubles the next damage taken** — from *any* source (attacks, hazardous terrain, *Wound*, etc.).
- **Consumed on that first hit** — *Brittle* is immediately removed after the damage is doubled.
- **Healing also removes it** — getting healed before taking that hit clears *Brittle* with no damage doubling.
- **Cannot be stacked** — a figure already afflicted with *Brittle* cannot gain a second instance, though gaining it again resets its duration (no practical difference here since *Brittle* has no timed removal — it lasts until damage or healing).
- **Interaction with character damage** — the rulebook (chunk 125) confirms that card-loss damage negation applies *after* *Brittle* is factored in: "when a character would suffer any amount of damage (after ward or brittle is applied), they must either reduce their red hit point dial by that amount or negate the damage."
